fr:mainmatter.html:p: The mutual information between two random variables X, Y is defined with respect to their joint distribution. Namely, it is the Kullback-Liebler Divergence between the joint distribution p(x,y) and the product of the marginals p(x)p(y) i.e. H(X || Y) = \mathbb {E}_{x,y \sim p(x,y)}\left [\log \frac {p(x, y)}{p(x)p(y)}\right ]

The talk starts discussing Ben's amazing paper DIAYN. An idea that has become incredibly popular in unsupervised / self-supervised RL is learning conditional policies. In Contrastive Reinforcement Learning, the learn a goal-conditioned policy and value function i.e. \pi _{\theta }(s \mid g), \quad Q_{\psi }(s, a, g) However, we are not limited to goals. In successor-feature-based frameworks, one learns a task vector \mathbf {w} such that r(s, a) = \phi (s,a) \cdot \mathbf {w}, where \phi is a lower dimensional representation. To learn the task vector (assuming you have \phi which is a whole other issue), you can recover \mathbf {w} by solving the linear regression \min _{\psi } \|r(s,a) - \phi (s,a) \cdot \mathbf {w}_{\psi }\|^{2} One can then learn a task conditioned policy \pi _{\theta }(s \mid \mathbf {w}). This is precisely the idea in USFA.

Getting back to DIAYN (get used to these tangents), instead of a task or goal, they condition on a skill \mathbf {z}. However, unlike a goal which is just a state in the environment, or a task which can be learned via regression as above, it is unclear how to learn this "skill" \mathbf {z}.
In this paper, they consider a skill as something distinguishable by a third party. What does this mean:

html:div.html:em: If you have a skill, then a discriminator observing you perform your skill should be able to tell you which skill you performed.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Crucially, the discriminator does not know which actions you took when performing this skill. Namely, they must be able to identify the skill purely based on the states you visited when performing that skill. Mathematically, this can be roughly translated as maximizing the mutual information I^{\pi }(\mathbf {z}; \tau ), where \tau is a trajectory of states (s_0, s_1, \ldots , s_T) \sim \pi visited by your policy \pi . Using our previous knowledge of mutual information, we can rewrite this as I^{\pi }(\mathbf {z}, \tau ) = H(\mathbf {z}) - H(\mathbf {z} \mid \tau ), where H is the entropy. Since H(\mathbf {z}) is a constant, this is essentially, \min _{\mathbf {z}} H(\mathbf {z} \mid \tau ) which very intuitively corresponds to the notion that \mathbf {z} should be able to be predicted by \tau . Formally, this is captured by the fact that the entropy is minimized i.e. 0 when \mathbf {z} = f(\tau ) for some invertible f.
Kellen Kanarios2025630Remark Recall that entropy is actually defined on the PDF of a random variable X. Therefore, minimizing the entropy over \mathbf {z} is actually finding a distribution p(\mathbf {z}).
To learn the distribution p(\mathbf {z}), they utilize the ELBO.

learning a discrimator distribution q_{\theta } such that \max _{\theta } q_{\theta }(\mathbf {z} \mid s_t), where we can easily learn this via maximum likelihood estimation because we are given s_{t + 1} \sim \pi (s_{t} \mid \mathbf {z})
\mathcal {L}_N from Representation Learning with Contrastive Predictive Coding maximizes a lower bound on the Mutual Information between x_{t + k} and c_t.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: All we must do is plug \frac {p(x \mid c)}{p(x)} back into the objective. \begin {align*} \mathcal {L}_{\mathbb {N}}^{\text {opt}}&=-\,\mathbb {E}\log \left [\frac {\frac {p(x_{t+k}|c_{t})}{p(x_{t+k})}}{\frac {p(x_{t+k}|c_{t})}{p(x_{t+k})}+\sum _{x_{j}\in X_{\text {neg}}}\frac {p(x_{j}|c_{t})}{p(x_{j})}}\right ] \\ &=\mathbb {E}\log \left [1+\frac {p(x_{t+k})}{p(x_{t+k}|c_{t})}\sum _{x_{j}\in X_{\text {neg}}}\frac {p(x_{j}|c_{t})}{p(x_{j})}\right ] \\ &\approx \mathbb {E}\log \left [1+\frac {p(x_{t+k})}{p(x_{t+k}|c_{t})}(N-1)\,\mathbb {E}\,\frac {p(x_{j}|c_{t})}{p(x_{j})}\right ] \\ &=\mathbb {E}\log \left [1+\frac {p(x_{t+k})}{p(x_{t+k} \mid c_t)}N\right ] \\ &\geq \mathbb {E} \log \left [\frac {p(x_{t + k})}{p(x_{t + k} \mid c_t)}N \right ] \\ &= - I(x_{t + k}, c_t) + \log N \end {align*}

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The KL-divergence between two probability distributions is defined as \mathrm {KL}(p || q) = \mathbb {E}_{x \sim p}\left [\log \frac {p(x)}{q(x)}\right ] = \sum _{x \in \mathcal {X}} p(x) \log \frac {p(x)}{q(x)}
